subject: Are AI tools making interviews harder, not easier?
body:
Here's what happens when you leave Cursor on during an interview.

View in browser (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VV_Hrj2s5-_hW8vV9Wy280D23W3S9mTt5L02-PN8JCsF25m_5PW7Y9pgv6lZ3mRW6PTZ5f8Z8plvW5strfh8zNQKpW7C2Kq23gFHWhW31GPdH9cjJWmW1x9Rpm7sBhWNW24yPnR6Y5mTrW4jlpzd39B5byW6lzrCw25L4bXW7z7ZhL7JxrFNW5WCCh-3rzjtBW2sGkC670YtqpW5J3GVs28x--HW4Qp4-j8pqzvcN2z31p5ySY7lW8Sgjrt8NknSrW3g2_VH3sVvxHW94V-0C30c1-MW7t6vMt59WGFxW2YYm_L7f4KmvW9fh24V8LjxCtW4TvkS54QdsqHW3gHtSN4CLDLnW4jyCk95dkbc9W8Kq89n4H66jXW3dLq4Q4W5zv_W6DrqWV40t2kWF6L9KXB_yNJW3CBL6_7XlpMGN6pd046XVp2xW1hF-px304DqwW322hZ97jxjC8W3jPcbt6wsbMdW26-NZr7vR_M-W6R671S8WPXyBW95GHx66_Vj8FW6RF0wJ37Nl_nN3Zl9RBn3hqlW1p5bFd1xqTXpW5jsTJ32RsHQRW8JLZcc4X2_PhW6lPdH-10tdbNW8Gt2v61FbM67f8_pqKx04 )

Денис,

One of our favorite guest experts, Brian Jenney (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VV_Hrj2s5-_hW8vV9Wy280D23W3S9mTt5L02-PN8JCsF23prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3plW8FGZVv5gyn_RVfL1Q57qZ8W-N1N4LwhVWfCSW5BdZr92KZPNwW7FDBhS7XkWHZW6Qm_ym5LCTgJW7kmkLt1LnnL6W3qZ1qf5dMnXBW2wlzCY2CSBcQW4SjbY-6v5sqbW7Zg2Ky5_RG1PW47XmcC624nz6W9kYhWQ8s_n4KW2tVw5j8CVPkVW4tdJwF8S8xcPW4HqCrr1vk3W0W5YSdkS4KFgs5W2bNVrx2sQNFxW7gWMmd27hM9gW6S67Xw5xlc77W5k01PC6CdhCPW3tk4kX7M8Sj9W8nl1RK5zpNcWW62p4Qp89cNb9W1csWs14HR4CwVtpNlF8vTNS9f8KnSh204 ) , is back — and this is for anyone who thinks AI will compromise tech interviews.

In today’s piece, Brian shares a real story: a CTO told him to keep Cursor open during a live coding interview… and the interview got harder, not simpler. He'll tell you exactly why in just a moment.

So if you’re using AI (and let’s be real, most developers are), this is the playbook for staying credible in interviews, without getting flagged as a “prompt jockey.”

👉 Our 3 million learner celebration wraps up tomorrow. That means now is your last chance to take advantage of 50% off (or more) all Educative subscriptions. (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VV_Hrj2s5-_hW8vV9Wy280D23W3S9mTt5L02-PN8JCsFl3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3mSW8MylhD7NhtHPN39kq6qSd8_tW17qtj42BSJsWW4PrB9665rg5jW52TGpY5mZZddW2DPXG82VKxnCW5JhgnR9lcWbjW7G4pLw12w4ntN4RK9jHVft85N7Pnc7fJLYwhW6LzzKk1mJJ0MW3L1N9M8M4_BQW7321Yd8m9Fq-W4QL1qV3CPMzTW7H6FDW3Lf7KBW6Km-tQ1xbL0dW5scXZQ28lP3MV1B2f-6HwD6SV1jBzs5zc6bKW7tY1q84FsC34W8dDQTt6mVrD5W21BbmT7CCytpW3LBtmt5ySP5XW8sgHW66hxckXW9g4sQk1YhPxrW63jDlz7BNT7mW4rLcDZ6XYsBNW6G2-sZ5JBR4Mf90xmpK04 )

Now over to Brian!

The CTO Told Me to Leave Cursor On. The Interview Got Harder.

“Are you using any AI tools we should know about?”

“No.”

“Great, can you explain what that first line of code is doing?”

“You still there?”

We never saw that candidate again. By this point, it was clear he had lied about his experience and very likely could not write a for loop on his own.

By the way, we explicitly allowed AI usage during this interview.

This was the second candidate so far who caught cheating.

Six months before this incident, I was at the other end of the interview table for this same company. During my live coding interview, I instinctively switched from Cursor to VS Code.

The CTO stopped me.

“No — leave Cursor on. We want to see how you solve this with AI.”

I thought the interview would be easier.

It wasn’t.

“Why did you accept that code?”

“Why didn’t you accept that code?”

“What is the benefit of your approach over Cursor here?”

“How do you determine what to use vs not use?”

“What’s your approach to generating code with AI?”

Not only was I solving difficult coding challenges, but I was also defending my choices and explaining my philosophy on prompting while doing it.

Your next coding interview is increasingly likely to look like this.

Roughly 84% of developers now use or plan to use AI tools in their workflow.

This means pretending you don’t use AI - or worse, pretending you understand what AI produced when you don’t, is becoming one of the fastest ways to fail an interview.

Here are 5 ways to increase your surface area for luck in your next interview in the AI era.

1. Stop Refusing Out of Pride

Some candidates deliberately avoid AI because they think it earns brownie points.

It doesn’t.

Saying things like:

“I’d rather do this without AI”

“I want to show I can solve it myself”

“I don’t really use these tools”

actively works against you.

If the company uses AI internally - and most do - then refusing to use it is like saying you are opposed to using GitHub.

Most interviewers won’t debate you.

They’ll just note it… and move on.

2. Say What You’re Doing and Why You’re Doing It

Silence is suspicious.

This isn’t fair, and it’s not how we usually work.

This is interview reality.

Verbalize your thoughts. This is especially important in an era where cheating is rampant.

If you’re using AI, narrate your intent:

“I’m going to ask Cursor to scaffold this, but I’ll tweak the API shape.”

“I’m accepting this part, but I don’t like how it handles errors.”

“This gets us 80% there, now let me adjust this logic for edge cases.”

Your decision-making process is what separates prompt jockeys from software developers.

3. Treat AI Output as a First Draft, Not an Answer

The fastest way to fail is to accept AI output without modification.

Instead, build a habit of immediately asking yourself:

- Does this match the constraints?

- Is this over-engineered?

- Would I write this in a real codebase?

Even small changes signal competence:

- Renaming variables

- Simplifying logic

- Removing unnecessary abstractions

Again, they want to see how you think through problems, not how sexy your prompts are.

If you want structured practice building real problem-solving skills (instead of just pasting AI output), pattern-based prep like Grokking Coding Interview Patterns (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VV_Hrj2s5-_hW8vV9Wy280D23W3S9mTt5L02-PN8JCsCz5m_5PW50kH_H6lZ3mNW8jRQ5M1s17Z8VB5jPW3CvShnW39C7Fy981y7TW2KNkv_3zsl6WW5pff_v43SjfvW63yV1T6SvQLzVtqWtk1KWTKDW9bjcDH7S0SZnW7PXQPV8L6XC3W6_jbRH296yF5N3csJX1S3YpLW1dkgqJ3Yd4k8W13tKVd7l6y_6W1nN-5d204PwPW6Z99xW31p1qyW3ygV-14nh52CW5XZwth5Gd-dLVDMV_g7FQ89sW6zNsl71wxgRsW2P7p8y7DvJGHW5hTtJL5bvV99W6qBR7y899rgRW982LN49cFfm8Vynvtl8jXP9QW3wmWs-7scBNYVv0_dr2S-HD9W3-pNbm35JR4MW1nrpnJ4HDWlLW107q5F73LrBTW7rV_Cp5vNMQjVDCpb91m3shmVvZtX643J-Zff5_VrtK04 )  is one of the best ways to train how you think through problems.

4. Be Able to Explain Every Line You Keep

This is non-negotiable.

If the code stays on the screen, you should be able to explain:

- What it does

- Why it exists

- What you’d change with more time

You don’t need perfect explanations. Just be honest. It’s cheaper and will help you sleep better at night.

Saying:

“I don’t love this part, but it works for now”

is infinitely better than pretending.

5. Don’t Optimise for Passing the Challenge — Optimise for Being Hirable

Here’s the trap a lot of people fall into:

They think the goal is to get through the interview.

AI can likely solve whatever interview problem you throw at it — but they’re not hiring Claude… they’re hiring you (for now).

Modern interviews, especially AI-assisted ones, are closer to:

“Would I want to work with this person? Do I trust their judgment?”

This means:

- Clear communication

- Reasoning through tradeoffs

- Honesty about uncertainty

No one expects perfection.

We’re all learning how to use these tools as we go.

And as you move toward more senior roles, interviews shift beyond coding into architecture and tradeoffs. Resources like Grokking Modern System Design Interview (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VV_Hrj2s5-_hW8vV9Wy280D23W3S9mTt5L02-PN8JCsCT5m_5PW5BWr2F6lZ3l0W1BprBm1L5bkvW3J3Std1PywD6W3-PFHp1fxVJCW8NCrpj6wMMPyN7XT7btJTybxW3-T1Fx41Hm4_W461rfK5KcXx2VYS6cd7Zf3JKV8KwrW2J887ZW7wH3N96V02sjW17L_m994K-TbW6mwyJc1sgDsZW4c85Vt7lgX-4W2sh-zD8zZchkW25z6YH2x8tylW2g0YkL7B3732W8pHDML3kSS7XW2pQzY3536kXjW3Y08Jd3XxKGwW5rCZk930p20XW5_yy6Z2gh487W4gb56x5X1PVsVcFHHJ7LBKvxW2dt2dS99n713W36SY-t19Jz07W1m5Nxd7mj2qTW3DMNmr7kJCHzW4NFmy28nD775W76L1nl8TQc9DW8g0sS37VBJnbW78qYx47t-f7qW7NQY_z7jzN9pW7GJ4Wh5hK9qTW8KW1WV1hkRFhdDZM2x04 )  are great for learning how to think through scalable systems,  the exact kind of thinking interviewers are probing for.

Old Habits Die Hard

We’re in a strange state.

Most developers use AI tools. Most companies expect us to use them. Yet tech interviews are struggling to keep pace with this new reality.

There is no playbook yet.

Your next interview could involve implementing a binary search tree in a Google Doc or prompting your way through building a React component.

You need to be prepared for both.

⚠️ And one more thing—Educative's 3M learner celebration ends tomorrow, so make sure to lock in your discount. (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VV_Hrj2s5-_hW8vV9Wy280D23W3S9mTt5L02-PN8JCsFl3prCCW8wLKSR6lZ3mSW8MylhD7NhtHPN39kq6qSd8_tW17qtj42BSJsWW4PrB9665rg5jW52TGpY5mZZddW2DPXG82VKxnCW5JhgnR9lcWbjW7G4pLw12w4ntN4RK9jHVft85N7Pnc7fJLYwhW6LzzKk1mJJ0MW3L1N9M8M4_BQW7321Yd8m9Fq-W4QL1qV3CPMzTW7H6FDW3Lf7KBW6Km-tQ1xbL0dW5scXZQ28lP3MV1B2f-6HwD6SV1jBzs5zc6bKW7tY1q84FsC34W8dDQTt6mVrD5W21BbmT7CCytpW3LBtmt5ySP5XW8sgHW66hxckXW9g4sQk1YhPxrW63jDlz7BNT7mW4rLcDZ6XYsBNW6G2-sZ5JBR4Mf90xmpK04 )

Happy Learning!

Brian Jenney (https://learn.educative.io/e3t/Ctc/RG+113/cz5H404/VV_Hrj2s5-_hW8vV9Wy280D23W3S9mTt5L02-PN8JCsF23prCCW7Y8-PT6lZ3plW8FGZVv5gyn_RVfL1Q57qZ8W-N1N4LwhVWfCSW5BdZr92KZPNwW7FDBhS7XkWHZW6Qm_ym5LCTgJW7kmkLt1LnnL6W3qZ1qf5dMnXBW2wlzCY2CSBcQW4SjbY-6v5sqbW7Zg2Ky5_RG1PW47XmcC624nz6W9kYhWQ8s_n4KW2tVw5j8CVPkVW4tdJwF8S8xcPW4HqCrr1vk3W0W5YSdkS4KFgs5W2bNVrx2sQNFxW7gWMmd27hM9gW6S67Xw5xlc77W5k01PC6CdhCPW3tk4kX7M8Sj9W8nl1RK5zpNcWW62p4Qp89cNb9W1csWs14HR4CwVtpNlF8vTNS9f8KnSh204 )
